summary: Jeff Atwood explains the three main LCD panel technologies (IPS, VA, and TN) and their respective tradeoffs in color reproduction, viewing angles, response time, and price. He notes that most consumer displays use TN panels because they're cheapest, allowing manufacturers to offer larger screens at competitive prices. From personal experience with both PVA and TN monitors, he observes that TN's most noticeable weakness is its severely limited vertical viewing angle, requiring users to keep their heads in a precise 'sweet spot.' While IPS panels offer the best quality, they're being pushed into expensive professional markets, leaving consumers with limited options.
key_points: Three LCD panel families exist: IPS (best quality, most expensive), VA (middle ground), and TN (cheapest, most common)
TN panels dominate the consumer market because their low cost enables larger screen sizes at competitive prices
TN displays suffer from severely limited vertical viewing angles, causing noticeable color intensity variations from top to bottom
IPS technology is being pushed into expensive 'pro' markets, often costing more than twice as much as alternatives
Emerging technologies like LED backlighting may eventually improve TN panels to reproduce 100% of the NTSC color gamut
money_quotes: Not all LCD panels are created equal.
Most panels these days are TN, which isn't much of a surprise; if a consumer has the choice between a 22" or 24" display at the same price, they're naturally going to go with the larger one.
Although TN displays can be quite good, they all suffer to some degree from the genetic defects of their TN family lineage.
You have to keep your head perfectly aligned in the tiny 'sweet spot' to see consistent vertical color on these displays.
IPS is widely considered the best all around LCD display technology, but it is rapidly being pushed into the vertical 'pro' graphics designer market due to the big jump in price.
The next time you set out to buy a LCD, be informed about the underlying display technology you're getting.
key_insight: Understanding the underlying panel technology (IPS, VA, or TN) is essential when buying an LCD monitor, as the technology determines fundamental tradeoffs in quality, viewing angles, and price that marketing specs often obscure.
